summary: In response to a massive npm supply-chain attack, DeVault outlines a comprehensive vision for how the JavaScript ecosystem could fix its broken dependency management — then concludes none of it will happen. He argues that micro-dependencies, lack of a standard library, and npm's fundamentally flawed design enable these attacks. He points to Linux distributions as proven models for secure software distribution. The post is structured as an extended hypothetical of what responsible action would look like, only to deliver a bitter punchline: the industry will settle for symbolic gestures and learn nothing.
key_points: The JavaScript ecosystem's sprawling micro-dependency trees are a structural vulnerability that enables supply-chain attacks at massive scale
JavaScript needs a real standard library (led by Google and Mozilla) to eliminate the need for trivial micro-packages like left-pad
Linux distributions' model of decoupling development from packaging, with curated maintainers and webs of trust, is the proven solution npm refuses to adopt
The same fundamental flaws exist in Cargo, PyPI, RubyGems, and other language-specific package managers
Despite decades of warnings and repeated incidents, the industry will only produce symbolic gestures like mandatory 2FA rather than structural reform
money_quotes: People have been sounding the alarm for years that this approach to dependency management is reckless and dangerous and broken by design.

It could leave behind its sprawling dependency trees full of micro-libraries, establish software distribution based on relationships of trust, and incorporate the decades of research and innovation established by more serious dependency management systems.
Recall that, ultimately, npm is GitHub is Microsoft, market cap $3 trillion USD.

By introducing universal signatures for packages of executable code, smaller channels and webs of trust, reproducible builds, and the many other straightforward, obvious techniques used by responsible package managers.
This would be a good future, but it's not the future that lies in wait for us.

Expect symbolic gestures – mandatory 2FA will be rolled out in more places, certainly, and the big players will write off meager donations in the name of "OSS security and resilience" in their marketing budgets.
No one will learn their lesson. This has been happening for decades and no one has learned anything from it yet.
This is the defining hubris of this generation of software development.
key_insight: The JavaScript ecosystem knows exactly how to fix its supply-chain security crisis — Linux distributions solved it decades ago — but institutional inertia and misaligned incentives guarantee that nothing meaningful will change.
